metadata.title: KAM Tori Seismic Periodicity and H³ Fault Network Geodesics for Earthquake Prediction Certificates
metadata.description: Tectonic fault systems are near-integrable Hamiltonians. OmegaFlow v13 KAMCheck() provides seismic stability certificate — KAM failure signals transition to chaotic regime (large ruptures). Theorem OG-GEO01: Lyapunov λ from H³ seismic waveform trajectory distinguishes stable creep (λ<0.001) from nucleating rupture (λ>0.01) providing 5-30 second early warning. Selberg zeta Z(s,Γ) for fault network counts closed rupture paths — formal catalog of resonant seismic cycles. κ=1.60225455 from ORCID 0009-0003-2911-444X. ZKP commitment block 40840.

data.title: KAM Tori Seismic Periodicity and H³ Fault Network Geodesics for Earthquake Prediction Certificates

metadata.title: H³ Geodesic Current Routing and KAM Tori Thermohaline Stability Certificates for Ocean Modeling
metadata.description: Ocean circulation is a near-integrable Hamiltonian with persistent KAM tori. KAMCheck() stability certificate: AMOC passes iff gcd(thermohaline,tidal)=1. Theorem OG-OCN01: Eddy kinetic energy E_k∝sinh²(κr) matching observed exponential decay in Poincaré disk model. H³ geodesics provide minimum-time ship routing in non-uniform flow fields. Lyapunov λ>0.01 signals chaotic mixing (upwelling) vs KAM-stable stratification — maps to harmful algal bloom prediction. κ=1.60225455 from ORCID 0009-0003-2911-444X. ZKP commitment block 40840.

data.title: H³ Geodesic Current Routing and KAM Tori Thermohaline Stability Certificates for Ocean Modeling

metadata.title: The Triadic Syndrome of Pregnancy. Preeclampsia in a New Paradigm.
metadata.description:
The Triadic Syndrome of Pregnancy. Preeclampsia in a New Paradigm. (Monographic Edition)

This monograph develops, in extended form, the conceptual framework presented in the previous versions. It proposes that the obstetric pathology of the gestational interface — preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, intrauterine fetal death, mirror syndrome (Ballantyne), placental abruption, HELLP, eclampsia — is not an organ disease, but the expression, on one of its slopes, of a relational imbalance described through three functional axes: metabolic demand, the supply of provisioning, and the variability of the response. The state of this imbalance is denoted Δ and is not read directly, but through the faces it takes when it meets a reactive response. The placenta-centric dyadic model, dominant since Redman’s two-stage articulation (1991), is reframed as the clinical projection of an underlying triadic process that has become molecularly observable over the last decade.

Conceptual refinement relative to the previous versions (v1–v3). The mapping of the three axes onto actors (the fetus = Demand, the placenta = Supply, the mother = Variability), used in the essay versions, is replaced here with a functional formulation: demand, supply, and the variability of the response are functions of the system, not persons. A disproportion of demand may arise from the growing fetus or from a twin pregnancy; supply, from placental and maternal capacity; and variability, from the biological reactivity that decides whether and on which slope the imbalance becomes visible. The C-O-V acronym of the previous versions is abandoned in favor of the direct description of the axes.

The structural contributions of the framework include: (1) the diagnosis of the structural tension of the dyadic model through eight catalogued clinical configurations (preeclampsia at term with a morphologically normal placenta, the FGR↔preeclampsia dissociation, unexplained IUFD with an antiangiogenic state, the hydatidiform mole, confined placental mosaicism with chromosome-specific phenotypes, twin pregnancy, mirror syndrome, placental abruption); (2) the spectral articulation on fifteen empirically documented axes — six maternal, five placental, four fetal — plus transversal operators (APS, SLE) and gradual modulators (chronic hypertension, pre-existing diabetes); (3) the supply-limited / demand-driven distinction as a temporal modulation of dominance over the course of gestation; (4) the central testable prediction — the silent configuration (high-demand, minimal-variability), a clinically silent fraction of the imbalance that escapes current screening; (5) the translational pathway through the FMF screening platform, with prospective studies specified operationally; (6) the evolutionary-populational dimension of variability, articulated through the KIR-HLA-C data (Hiby, Nakimuli) and population-stratified MoM calibration; (7) the criteria of falsification at three levels (explanatory adequacy, predictive commitment, architectural commitment).

Conceptual continuity. The framework originates in an earlier communication by one of the authors (Mureșan MB, ResearchGate, April 2015) under the acronym FPI (fetoplacental imbalance), which formulated the central intuition — a relational imbalance between fetal demand and placental supply — at a moment when the molecular substrate had not yet matured. The present systematic articulation integrates the fetal cfRNA evidence (Yoffe 2018; Munchel 2020; Moufarrej 2022; Castillo-Marco 2025; the Weiner program 2017–2026), the confined placental mosaicism data (Yong 2003; Eggenhuizen 2024; Thomsen 2024), the fetal McGinnis 2017 GWAS at the FLT1 locus, and the Thilaganathan-Melchiorre cardiovascular school.

The monographic version (May 2026) develops the content of the earlier essay versions (v1.0 RO, Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19885101; v2.0 EN, 10.5281/zenodo.20084943; v3, 10.5281/zenodo.20106209) into ten chapters: the history of the syndrome as a succession of frontiers of the observable (Ch. 1–2); the evolutionary grounding, from genetic conflict to phenotype (Ch. 3); the complete construction of the three vertices with their biology, their axes, and their biomarkers (Ch. 4–6); the mathematical formalization of the imbalance (δ = ln(C/O); disease as a temporal integral ∫δ dt; the phenotype as a product of the reactivity of the response and the positive component of the imbalance); the reading of each clinical entity through five coordinates (Ch. 7); and the consequences for screening, management, and nomenclature (Ch. 8–10). The Romanian original remains the canonical formulation.

Total: approx. 48,000 words (body), 109 pages, 10 chapters, 8 figures, 132 references (126 with PMID — 95%, 129 with a stable PMID/DOI identifier).

metadata.title: AVIDbase repository of biologically accurate and standardized nanobody-antigen complexes
metadata.description:
Abstract

Accurate structural data in a standarized format is one of the key factors behind the success of machine learning (ML)-based methods for protein design and structure prediction. However, their application to nanobody-antigen complexes has lower success rates compared to globular protein complexes, partly due to the limited amount of high-quality structural data. While several dedicated databases already exist, automated assembly pipelines frequently overlook various artifacts, which act as additional noise and limit the effectiveness of ML applications. Common issues include incorrect biological assembly, redundancy bias, inclusion of crystal contacts, strained geometry due to crystal packing, as well as missing density or post-translational modifications near the interface. To address these issues, we present AVIDbase (Antigen-VHH Interface Database), a highly curated dataset of nanobody-antigen structures. In addition to correcting structural artifacts, the dataset provides a nonredundant set of structures with standardized chain identifiers, harmonized metadata, and cleaned atomic coordinates in a ready-to-use format for ML applications.

Repository contents

AVIDbase is an annotated database of all nanobody-antigen interfaces found within publicly available crystal structures deposited in the PDB as of February 9th, 2026. The database contains nanobody complexes with protein and ordered peptide antigens.

The repository consists of 3 files:

  • AVIDbase.xlsx - an Excel file encompassing the final nanobody-antigen interface database. The first column contains a unique ID associated with a single complex structure. It consists of the following sheets:
    • AVIDbase-nr - subset of nonredundant complexes (determined based on identity of CDR1-3 sequence, antigen name, and antigen source organism). The nonredundant complexes were chosen based on ranking by resolution and interface integrity (i. e. interface proximity of missing density segments, posttranslational modifications, obligate cofactors or bound substrate).
    • AVIDbase-prot - subset of AVIDbase-nr containing complexes with high or medium interface integrity (no or negligible interface missing density, contacts with PTM species or cofactors) and with a crystallographic resolution ≤ 3.5 Å.
    • AVIDbase-low - remaining AVIDbase-nr complexes with low interface integrity and crystallographic resolution > 3.5 Å.
    • AVIDbase-r - redundant complexes not found in AVIDbase-nr. Contains redundant complexes found in the same PDB structure as the representative AVIDbase-nr complex (intra_redundancy column) or in a separate PDB structure (inter_redundancy column).
  • annotation_input_table_MANUAL.xlsx - an Excel file describing all automatically identified interfaces of nanobody structures in the PDB, subjected to manual review of both the crystal structure and associated literature. It represents the input file used to generate both the final AVIDbase tables and the standardized structures found in AVIDbase.zip. Complexes that were incorrectly assigned, complexes defined by incomplete biological assemblies, etc. were manually edited to ensure  Columns that were manually edited are highlighted in yellow:
    • is_edited - Boolean index determining whether specified complex was manually edited.
    • edit_comment - free text column used by the authors to provide additional context for certain modifications or exclusion of certain complexes.
    • keep - Boolean index determining whether specified complex is retained in the final database. Rows with keep=0 have an accompanying justification under edit_comment.
    • assembly_strategy - notes how the corresponding complex structure was obtained:
      • bio_assembly - from a given biological assembly specified in the corresponding PDB structure, with the specific ID noted under bio_assembly_id.
      • asym - from the asymmetric unit, with bio_assembly_id set to 0.
      • from_symmetry - via symmetry operations to obtain the unit cell.
    • ag_auth_chain_id - comma-separated list of chain IDs comprising the biologically relevant antigen assembly. Duplicate chain IDs are differentiated by a symmetry index, e.g. A_0,A_1.
    • expected_cofactors - comma-separated list of non-protein, non-glycan residue names expected in the structure.
    • ag_name - custom naming convention for the antigen, created to maximize harmonization between different PDB structures containing the same antigen under different names.
  • AVIDbase.zip - .zip file containing standardized .pdb structures belonging to each subset of AVIDbase, with the following layout:
    • AVIDbase-nr
      • AVIDbase-low - all structures listed in AVIDbase-low with no additional processing.
      • AVIDbase-prot - all structures listed in AVIDbase-prot, additionally processed by removing all noncanonical moieties (i. e. the protein-only subset).
      • AVIDbase-prot-relaxed - AVIDbase-prot structures additionally relaxed with Rosetta.
      • full - all structures listed in AVIDbase-nr with no additional processing.
    • AVIDbase-r
      • full - all structures listed in AVIDbase-r with no additional processing.

Available structural data

All nanobody-antigen structures are processed by:

  • renumbering nanobody under the IMGT scheme with ANARCII
  • removing N- and C-terminal expression tags
  • standardizing chain IDs:
    • nanobody chain ID is set to H, only one nanobody per structure,
    • antigen chain IDs are set in alphabetical order (e. g. A,B,C),
    • cofactor chain ID is set to X,
    • glycan chain IDs are set to lowercase equivalents of covalently linked antigen chain ID.
  • replacing all selenomethionine residues with methionines.

Rosetta-relaxed structures additionally:

  • are repacked and minimized, with mitigated crystallographic clashes,
  • contain explicit H-atoms optimizing hydrogen bonding,
  • include per-residue Rosetta beta_nov16 score terms at the end of the structure file.

data.title: AVIDbase repository of biologically accurate and standardized nanobody-antigen complexes

metadata.title: The Neutral Chaoiton: A Falsifiable Dark Matter Candidate from the Ouroboros Lagrangian Constraints from Existing Public Data and Projections for Near-Term Tests
metadata.description:
The nature of dark matter remains unknown after decades of null results for WIMPs
and axions. We present the neutral chaoiton – a stable, time‑periodic, localized
solution of the Ouroboros Lagrangian, a classical field theory dynamically
equivalent to a superrenormalizable bosonic quantum field theory (Werbos 2026,
Paper I). The theory has only three free parameters with no renormalization
subtractions. The neutral chaoiton carries no electromagnetic charge, interacts
with ordinary matter via a massive vector field (the J‑field), and has mass
m_χ = 0.460 MeV, mediator mass m_J = 0.618 MeV, and coupling C = 770 MeV·fm –
all derived from the same parameters that fit the electron. No new parameters
are introduced.

This version (v3a) substantially extends v2 by: (1) performing a systematic
MOSIS‑analogy analysis of existing public data constraints on the chaoiton,
identifying what is already excluded and what is not yet tested; (2) precisely
characterizing the J‑field coupling to ordinary nucleons, which is the critical
question for all direct detection and stellar cooling constraints; (3) identifying
the Bullet Cluster / LSST / eROSITA self‑interaction measurement as the strongest
near‑term falsification test; (4) using public Gaia Dark Shards data to predict
the chaoiton’s annual modulation signal (peak dates around June 1–7 and
December 1–7) – a key falsifiable prediction for a dedicated J‑field sensor;
(5) connecting the Ouroboros model to the observed population of galaxies
without dark matter (DF2, DF4, FCC 224), showing that the predicted
self‑interaction cross‑section provides a natural mechanism for the “bullet
dwarf” scenario; (6) clarifying that dark matter may be a mixture of chaoiton
particles and J‑field waves, with the ratio not predicted by the theory; and
(7) proposing a concrete sequence of no‑cost computational analyses using
public datasets that can establish the chaoiton’s viability before any new
funding or hardware access.

The self‑interaction cross‑section σ/m ∼ 0.1‑1 cm²/g is consistent with Bullet
Cluster bounds (Randall et al. 2008: σ/m < 1.37 cm²/g) and will be definitively
tested by LSST and eROSITA. The J‑field couples to nucleons via the same
mechanism responsible for Sawada’s anomalous long‑range nuclear force – meaning
the chaoiton is NOT a pure dark‑sector particle and its nucleon coupling must be
computed from the Ouroboros equations. This coupling computation is identified
as the most urgent theoretical task. The Gaia stream prediction gives a
concrete, falsifiable signature for a J‑field sensor, independent of the
coupling strength, assuming chaoitons dominate locally.
